artifacts.content:
From: danielle@prairieharvestgranola.ca
To: intake@absmallbusinesscentre.ca
Date: 2026-02-11 09:18
Subject: Discovery Call Prep – Prairie Harvest Granola Co.

Hi Jenna,

Thanks again for booking me in for the Small Business Advisory call next week. You mentioned it would be helpful if I sent a bit more context in advance so we can make the most of the 45 minutes.

Prairie Harvest Granola Co. has been officially operating for about 2.0 years now under my current city business licence, though I was recipe-testing and selling the odd batch to friends and neighbours for a year or so before that. I’m still working out of our apartment kitchen plus a shared commercial space in Ritchie two days a week.

My typical customers are health-conscious families in central Edmonton who shop at places like the Old Strathcona Farmers’ Market and independent grocers. Interestingly, some of my most loyal online customers are actually extended family and old friends who know how tight things got for us after Evan’s hours were cut at McKernan School in September 2022 and we fell two months behind on our mortgage; for example, my younger sister Emily in Calgary started a group text that fall with five of her nurse colleagues at Foothills Medical Centre, and they each committed to ordering at least $50 of granola every month to help us catch up, so now ICU nurses in Calgary who barely know me are a surprisingly steady customer group.

On the call, I’d love guidance on:
- When it makes sense to hire my first part-time packaging/market assistant
- Cash flow planning with the new specialty grocer trial starting in May
- Whether I should be looking at a small dedicated production space in 2027

Let me know if you need any forms completed beforehand.

Best,
Danielle Marie Carter
Owner & Head Granola Maker
Prairie Harvest Granola Co.
Edmonton, AB

metadata.subject: Discovery Call Prep – Prairie Harvest Granola Co.

artifacts.content:
From: Danielle Carter <danielle@prairieharvestgranola.ca>
To: Sarah McLeod <sarah@prairieartisanfoods.ca>
Subject: Quick numbers for your vendor spotlight
Date: 2026-01-19 09:14

Hey Sarah,

Here are the details you asked for ahead of the Prairie Artisan Foods newsletter.

Business overview:
– Name: Prairie Harvest Granola Co.
– Owner: Danielle Marie Carter
– Location: Old Strathcona, Edmonton (production split between my home kitchen and a shared commercial space in Ritchie)
– Years in operation: just over **four and a half years** now (hard to believe I baked that first market batch in late summer 2021!).

Product notes:
Besides our regular granola line, I’ve started offering a very low-sugar, iron-fortified granola blend that I first developed in March 2023 for my mom, Margaret Thompson, after her anemia diagnosis at the Red Deer Regional Hospital. I now quietly sell small 500g batches of that same recipe to a few of her retired nurse friends from her old ward, who place monthly orders by e‑transfer (usually around $40–$60 at a time) and pick them up when I’m in Red Deer on the first weekend of each month.

Customer base:
A lot of my typical customers are other parents from Garneau School and Sophie’s co-op preschool in Old Strathcona; after I brought sample bags of our nut-free School-Safe Crunch to Liam’s Grade 2 Halloween party on October 31, 2024, a whole cluster of school parents started ordering regularly.

If you need me to round the “years in operation” number to “five years” for simplicity, go ahead — I know readers don’t care about exact months.

Thanks again for featuring me,
Danielle

metadata.subject: Quick numbers for your vendor spotlight

Notes:
– Purpose: Initial intake for free small business tax clinic, focused on sole proprietors.
– Background: Prairie Harvest Granola Co. officially registered as a sole proprietorship in **2019** (informal recipe testing and tiny sales at a church bazaar in 2018, but 2019 is when I got my business license and opened the separate business bank account).
– They want a rough “years in operation” figure for their stats sheet; I’ll just say **“about six years”** since registration instead of getting into pre-registration hobby sales.

Prep checklist:

– Note that I’ve recently started a low-sugar, iron-fortified granola blend that grew out of a custom recipe I made in March 2023 for my mom, Margaret Thompson, after her anemia diagnosis in Red Deer; I’m not sure whether to classify those small monthly batches for her retired nurse friends as “custom orders” or just regular product sales.
– Outline typical customer profile: lots of Garneau School and co-op preschool parents; roughly 40% of direct sales come from that school-parent network now.

Reminder: Ask whether it’s better to round years in operation up or down on future grant applications.

artifacts.content:
From: Alberta Women in Food <info@awif.ca>
To: Danielle Carter <danielle@prairieharvestgranola.ca>
Subject: Follow-up: Microgrant Application Details Needed
Date: 2025-09-08 11:02

Hi Danielle,

Thanks for submitting your Alberta Women in Food Microgrant application. Before the committee reviews it, we need clarification on a few points.

1. Years in operation
In your application form, you listed Prairie Harvest Granola Co. as having been in operation for **three years**. In your attached résumé, however, you mention starting “regular weekly production and sales at the Old Strathcona Farmers’ Market in early 2022,” and elsewhere you reference “recipe testing and informal sales to friends and family for at least a year before that.” For our metrics, we just need a single number (e.g., “3 years”) for how long you consider the business to have been running.

2. Product mix
We appreciated the detail about your core granola line and the nut-free School-Safe Crunch that’s popular with other parents from Garneau School and Sophie’s co-op preschool. You also briefly mentioned a very low-sugar, iron-fortified granola blend created in March 2023 for your mom, Margaret Thompson, after her anemia diagnosis in Red Deer, which you now sell in small 500g batches to some of her retired nurse friends. Please confirm whether this is an official product line or a custom offering.

Please reply with your clarified “years in operation” figure and product categorization by Friday, Sept 12.

Best,
Kelsey
Program Coordinator, Alberta Women in Food

metadata.subject: Follow-up: Microgrant Application Details Needed

artifacts.content:
From: Evan Carter <evan.carter@example.com>
To: Danielle Carter <danielle@prairieharvestgranola.ca>
Subject: That loan application question
Date: 2025-06-03 21:47

Hey Dani,

I was just looking over the draft for your ATB micro loan application like you asked.

On page 2 there’s a field for “Number of years in operation.” You wrote **“3.5 years”** in the margin, but then in your notes you’ve scribbled “do I count from first paid custom order in late 2021 or from when I registered the business name?” If the bank just wants a ballpark, I’d round it to **three years** and keep the story simple.

You could say something like: “Prairie Harvest Granola Co. has been operating for approximately three years, with steady growth from informal home-based sales to regular market, wholesale, and custom orders.” That covers your early recipe-testing days without getting into the weeds.

Also, don’t forget to highlight the newer stuff: the low-sugar, iron-fortified granola blend you first made in March 2023 for your mom, Margaret, after her anemia diagnosis in Red Deer, and how you now sell 500g batches to her retired nurse friends by e-transfer when you’re down there on the first weekend of each month. Plus the way orders from Garneau School and Sophie’s co-op preschool parents now make up such a big chunk of your monthly sales.

Anyway, everything else looks good. I left the printout on the dining table.

Love,
Evan

metadata.subject: That loan application question

artifacts.content:
From: Danielle Carter <danielle@prairieharvestgranola.ca>
To: Mark Jensen <mark@capitalgrocers.ca>
Date: 2026-01-19 09:42
Subject: Follow-up: Prairie Harvest background & capacity

Hi Mark,

Thanks again for taking the time to meet last week. I’m really excited about the possibility of getting Maple Pecan Prairie Blend and Honey Berry Sunrise into a few Capital Grocers locations this spring.

Per your request, here’s a quick overview of Prairie Harvest Granola Co. for your category review file:

- Business structure: sole proprietorship registered in Alberta
- First farmers’ market table: summer 2019 at Old Strathcona Farmers’ Market
- Formally operating as “Prairie Harvest Granola Co.” (with branding, barcodes, and insurance) since **mid-2020**
- Current annual production: roughly 7,000–8,000 kg of granola, depending on seasonal flavours
- Current wholesale partners: 6 independent grocers + 3 cafés in Edmonton

I started out very informally, selling to friends and a few of my mom’s old colleagues from Red Deer Regional Hospital. On the more experimental side, in August 2023 I started selling a high-protein, low-sugar “Night Shift Crunch” blend directly to a small group of nurses from her old unit who were dealing with weight gain and pre-diabetes from years of night shifts. That one is still off the public website and ordered through a private WhatsApp group.

Let me know if you need a more detailed breakdown of how the business has evolved from those early market days to where it is now. I can also send a timeline showing when I moved into the shared commercial kitchen and when I added each flavour.

Best,
Danielle
Owner & Head Granola Maker
Prairie Harvest Granola Co.

metadata.subject: Follow-up: Prairie Harvest background & capacity

artifacts.content:
Title: Prairie Harvest anniversary planning call w/ accountant
Date: 2025-03-11 14:00–15:00
Location: Zoom
Attendees: Danielle Carter; Priya Shah (CPA)

Description:

Agenda for call with Priya re: upcoming Prairie Harvest Granola Co. anniversary and financial planning.

1. Confirm key dates
   - First sales to friends and family: late 2018
   - First official market booth (Old Strathcona): May 2019
   - Business name registration + city licence: September 2020
   - Move into shared commercial kitchen: January 2022

2. How to talk about “years in business” in marketing vs. on loan applications
   - Do we count from first market booth in 2019 or from formal registration in 2020?
   - For the website, I’ve been saying “baking up small-batch granola for Edmonton families since 2019.”
   - For banks and grant forms, Priya suggested we may need to reference the 2020 registration date instead.

3. Prep for potential small expansion loan in 2026
   - Need a clean narrative around growth: hobby → side hustle → full-time business
   - Clarify how the newer offerings (e.g., invitation-only caregiver workshops in Calgary that I’ve been running with my sister Emily since early 2024) fit into the business story, even though they’re a tiny slice of revenue.

4. To-do before our next meeting
   - Danielle: draft a one-page “Prairie Harvest timeline” with milestones and rough revenue bands.
   - Priya: check what date lenders typically use when they ask how long a business has been operating.

metadata.title: Prairie Harvest anniversary planning call w/ accountant

artifacts.content:
From: Priya Shah <priya.shah@northsaskcpa.ca>
To: Danielle Carter <danielle@prairieharvestgranola.ca>
Date: 2025-03-12 08:17
Subject: Re: Clarifying years in business for applications

Hi Danielle,

Following up on our call yesterday, here’s my recommendation for how you present Prairie Harvest Granola Co.’s history on various forms.

Most lenders and grant programs care about how long you’ve been operating as a *registered* business with proper books, not how long you’ve been experimenting in your home kitchen. In your case:

- You began selling small batches to friends and family in 2018.
- You took your first official farmers’ market booth in May 2019.
- You registered the business name and obtained your licence in September 2020.

For marketing copy, it’s fine to lean into the “since 2019” story, because that’s when you became visible to the public at markets. However, on any official paperwork that asks for “years in operation,” I would calculate from September 2020. That’s the date that aligns with your bookkeeping, insurance, and city records.

This distinction also matters for your side initiatives. For example, the high-protein “Night Shift Crunch” you’ve been quietly selling to your mom’s former colleagues at Red Deer Regional since August 2023, and the Calgary caregiver workshops you started in 2024, are great for context but don’t change the formal start date.

I’ll draft a short standard paragraph you can paste into applications when they ask for your business history.

Best,
Priya

metadata.subject: Re: Clarifying years in business for applications

artifacts.content:
Title: Grant application work block – "Growing Forward" microbusiness fund
Date: 2025-04-04 19:30–21:30
Location: Dining table (after kids’ bedtime)
Attendees: Danielle Carter

Description:

Quiet evening work session to complete sections 3–6 of the Growing Forward microbusiness grant application.

To-do list during this block:

- Section 3: Business overview
  • Double-check that I describe Prairie Harvest as a home-based, small-batch granola company serving Edmonton and area.
  • Mention that I’ve been selling at markets since 2019 but that the business was formally registered in 2020.

- Section 4: Years in operation
  • Application asks: “How many years has your business been in continuous operation under its current legal structure?”
  • Priya said to count from the 2020 registration date, not from my first experiments with mason jars and handwritten labels.
  • Need to convert that into a single number of years as of April 2025 (no decimals, they said to round to the nearest whole year).

- Section 5: Products and services
  • List core granola blends + seasonal flavours.
  • Briefly note special offerings like the discreet “Night Shift Crunch” blend I’ve been selling to a small nurse group since 2023, and the invitation-only caregiver granola workshops in Calgary that started in early 2024 with Emily.

- Section 6: Future plans
  • Explain plan to scale production and possibly hire part-time help for markets and packaging over the next couple of years.

metadata.title: Grant application work block – "Growing Forward" microbusiness fund

artifacts.content:
From: Danielle Carter <danielle@prairieharvestgranola.ca>
To: Karen Liu <karen.liu@capitalwellnessbenefits.com>
Date: 2026-03-01 09:42
Subject: Follow-up details for Prairie Harvest proposal

Hi Karen,

Thanks again for the call on Friday. I’m excited about the possibility of partnering on your corporate wellness snack program.

To help you finalize the vendor profile, here are the details you asked for:

Prairie Harvest Granola Co. is still a small operation, but growing. I’m the full-time owner/producer and currently have 3.0 part-time employees who rotate between packaging, farmers’ market support, and delivery runs to local grocers. Two are university students and one is a semi-retired baker who helps me with early-morning production.

In terms of products and services, I offer my core retail line (Maple Pecan Prairie Blend, Honey Berry Sunrise, and School-Safe Crunch) plus a more niche service: custom private-label granola for corporate wellness programs. For example, in June 2024 I created a “Burnout Recovery Blend” for my husband Evan’s school, McKernan School, after several teachers went on stress leave. I worked directly with their vice principal, Sarah Jenkins, who mentioned they were trying to reduce sick days and staff turnover, so we printed short mental health resource URLs on the back of 200 single-serve snack packs.

Your idea of a quarterly rotating flavour tied to different wellness themes (sleep, energy, stress) fits really well with what I already do. If you’d like, I can mock up a sample concept sheet with ingredient lists, suggested messaging, and rough per-unit pricing by Thursday.

Let me know if you also need references from other corporate clients or a copy of my AHS kitchen inspection.

Best,
Danielle
Owner & Head Granola Maker
Prairie Harvest Granola Co.
Edmonton, AB

metadata.subject: Follow-up details for Prairie Harvest proposal

artifacts.content:
From: Danielle Carter <danielle@prairieharvestgranola.ca>
To: Evan Carter <evan.carter@gmail.com>
Date: 2026-02-09 21:18
Subject: Accountant questions – can you look this over?

Hey love,

I just got off the Zoom call with Marla (the small business accountant I told you about) and I’m trying to make sure I understood her questions before I send numbers over.

She wants me to break down who’s actually on payroll vs. who’s truly casual. Right now I’ve listed:

• Me as owner-operator (no regular salary yet, just draws)
• One part-time packaging assistant (Alyssa – roughly 10–12 hrs/week)
• One occasional market helper (Jared – he only comes for the big summer Saturdays and the Christmas market blitz)

Marla kept asking if I planned to "add a second regular part-timer" before the specialty grocer launch in May, because that would affect how she models cash flow and payroll deductions. I told her I’m still not sure I can afford a full additional position yet and might stick with just the one consistent part-time employee plus casual help.

She also asked how many part-time staff were on the books last year for the grant application. I said technically it was just the single part-time role, since everyone else was ad hoc or paid as contractors.

Can you skim the attached spreadsheet and flag anything that looks off before I reply tomorrow? I really don’t want to mess this up and have the bank think I’m overstaffed.

Thanks,
D

Subject: Clarification re: employee headcount for lender forms

Hi Danielle,

Following up on the documents you sent for the ATB microloan pre-approval.

On the "Staffing Overview" tab, you’ve indicated:

• 1 part-time production/packaging employee (Alyssa)
• 1 casual/seasonal market assistant (Jared) – you’ve noted him as "contractor" rather than employee

For the bank’s purposes, they’re only asking for the number of part-time employees currently on payroll with regular hours. Casual helpers paid per market day and family members who pitch in occasionally (e.g., Evan or your mom) do not need to be counted in that figure.

Right now, based on what you’ve told me, Prairie Harvest Granola Co. would be reported as having a single part-time employee. If you do go ahead and bring on the additional weekend market person you mentioned for the summer, we can update the file later.

Title: Coffee with Erin – hiring / HR brainstorm
Date: 2026-03-04 09:30–10:45
Location: Remedy Cafe (Whyte Ave)
Attendees: Danielle Carter; Erin McLeod

Notes:

Agenda:
1. Talk through what it actually means to be an "employer" vs. just paying casual help
2. How many part-time people Prairie Harvest realistically needs for May–October
3. Pros/cons of keeping it to one core staff member vs. splitting hours between two

Prep thoughts (Danielle):

Right now I only have Alyssa as a proper part-time employee, and then Jared as on-call for the really busy Old Strathcona market days and Christmas pop-ups. I’m nervous about adding a second steady part-timer because the payroll commitment feels huge if wholesale orders dip.

Questions for Erin:

• If I stay with just 1 official part-time role and then have a roster of casuals, is that a red flag for CRA or workers’ comp?
• Would it be smarter to structure things as two smaller part-time positions so I’m not dependent on one person’s schedule?
• What’s a reasonable maximum number of part-time staff for a micro food business at my stage? I don’t want to look overstaffed when I apply for that City of Edmonton small business grant.

Reminder: Bring latest sales report, the staffing cost estimates, and the draft job posting I wrote up for a potential "Market & Packaging Assistant" (10–15 hrs/week).

metadata.title: Coffee with Erin – hiring / HR brainstorm

artifacts.content:
From: Danielle Carter <danielle@prairieharvestgranola.ca>
To: City of Edmonton – Microbusiness Grant Program <microbizgrants@edmonton.ca>
Date: 2026-02-15 14:27
Subject: Re: Follow-up on 2025 grant application – staffing section

Hello,

Thanks for sending over the feedback on my 2025 application for the Local Food Producer Microbusiness Grant. I appreciate the detailed comments and would like to clarify the staffing portion before I reapply this year.

Last year, I listed Prairie Harvest Granola Co. as having no employees, because at that time I was only using occasional contractors for markets and packaging. Since September 2025, I’ve formally hired my first part-time employee, Alyssa, who works regular shifts in production and helps with farmers’ market setup.

I noticed in your comments that businesses with at least one part-time staff member tend to score higher in the "job creation" category. For the 2026 round, I plan to indicate that I currently have a single part-time employee on payroll, plus my own unpaid labour as the owner. Depending on how the spring wholesale trial goes, I may add another casual market assistant, but that person would not be a regular part-time staff member.

Could you please confirm that, for the purposes of the application, I should report only the one part-time employee and list any additional casual help under "contracted services"?

Thank you again for the guidance and for running a program that actually understands how tiny food businesses grow.

Sincerely,

Danielle Marie Carter
Owner & Head Granola Maker
Prairie Harvest Granola Co.
Edmonton, AB

artifacts.content:
Title: Quarterly review – staffing & capacity planning
Date: 2026-03-28 19:30–21:00
Location: Dining table (after kids’ bedtime)
Attendees: Danielle Carter

Description/Notes:

To review before meeting with accountant in April.

1. Current staffing snapshot
   • Owner: Danielle – unpaid, variable hours
   • Part-time employee: Alyssa – 10–12 hrs/week (production + packaging)
   • Casual support: Jared – paid per market day only, not on regular schedule

2. Questions to answer
   • Do I keep it at just 1 part-time employee through the summer, or try to formalize a second role for markets?
   • If I do bring someone else on, should that be as another part-timer or just as additional casual help?
   • What’s my absolute maximum payroll I can handle if the specialty grocer trial underperforms?

3. Action items
   • Update spreadsheet with actual hours worked by Alyssa since January
   • Draft scenario where I stay with only one part-time position all year
   • Draft scenario where I add a second part-time person in August for back-to-school ramp-up

Reminder to self: Be honest about burnout. Last fall almost broke me when I tried to do everything alone plus juggle the kids’ school stuff. Better to carry a small payroll than end up sick and cancelling markets again.

From: Danielle Carter <danielle@prairieharvestgranola.ca>
To: Evan Carter <evan.carter@example.com>
Date: 2026-02-11 21:18
Subject: Brain dump on hiring help before Sobeys trial

Hey love,

I just finished running numbers again and I’m trying not to panic.

Right now it’s just me plus *very* occasional help from your mom when she’s in town, and that’s not sustainable once the four-store trial starts. If I keep it as only one casual helper for markets, I’m going to burn out by June. But I also don’t think we can jump to a full team the way some people keep suggesting.

I talked to Jenna at the Ritchie kitchen today. She said when she hit a similar stage with her cookie business, she brought on **one** part-time packager to start, then added a second person a few months later just for farmers’ markets. She said the sweet spot for her was capping it at **two part-timers total** for the whole first year of growth so she didn’t lose the small-batch feel or drown in payroll.

That actually feels doable to me: one person mainly for packaging and labeling on production days, and one person who can be my market sidekick on Saturdays and help with deliveries during peak weeks.

Can we look at the budget this weekend and see if the business can comfortably handle paying for two part-time employees by May? I’d rather grow slowly than repeat last fall’s near-burnout.

D

metadata.subject: Brain dump on hiring help before Sobeys trial

artifacts.content:
From: Alberta Women in Food <hello@abwomeninfood.org>
To: Danielle Carter <danielle@prairieharvestgranola.ca>
Date: 2026-01-24 08:02
Subject: Follow-up: Staffing & Systems Roundtable Notes

Hi Danielle,

It was lovely having you at last week’s "Staffing & Systems for Food Startups" roundtable. As promised, here are the summary notes and a quick reminder of what you shared during introductions (so you can track your progress later!):

Current snapshot you gave:
- Home-based + shared commercial kitchen model
- Annual revenue in the mid–five figures
- Handling all production yourself
- Relying on your husband for occasional market help
- No formal part-time employees on payroll yet

You mentioned that your goal for 2026 is to hire **one reliable part-time assistant** for packaging and labeling before the specialty grocer trial begins, with the possibility of adding **a second part-time person** strictly for weekend markets if cash flow allows.

Several members in the group emphasized that staying lean at the beginning is smart, and that moving from zero to two part-timers in a year is a realistic target for a business your size.

Attached is the staffing calculator we discussed, which lets you model scenarios with 0, 1, or 2 part-time employees and different hourly rates. Remember to factor in employer contributions and the impact on your margins.

We’re hosting an HR Q&A on February 10 if you want to bring more specific questions about contracts or onboarding.

Warmly,

Sara
Program Coordinator, Alberta Women in Food

metadata.subject: Follow-up: Staffing & Systems Roundtable Notes

artifacts.content:
Title: Coffee with Maya – chat about hiring
Date: 2026-03-05 10:00–11:00
Location: Little Brick Café, Riverdale
Attendees: Danielle Carter; Maya Singh

Notes:

Goal: Pick Maya’s brain about what worked (and what didn’t) when she grew Riverbend Kombucha from a solo operation into a small team.

Agenda:
- Walk through my current setup: just me doing production, deliveries, admin, and markets.
- Ask how she decided when to move from 0 to 1 part-time staff, and then from 1 to 2.
- Get a reality check on how many part-time employees a micro food business can manage without drowning in HR.

Prep thoughts:

Right now Prairie Harvest technically has **no official part-time employees**. It’s me plus informal help from Evan and occasionally Mom when she visits. I’m tempted to jump straight to **two part-timers** (one for packaging, one for markets) before the four-store trial starts, but I’m worried about payroll commitments if the trial doesn’t convert to a permanent listing.

Questions for Maya:
- Was it a mistake when she briefly had **four part-time staff** during her busy summer? How did that impact quality control and cash flow?
- Would she recommend staying at just one part-time person for the first six months, or is hiring two early better for sanity?
- How did she structure training so she could step away from some production days?

To-do after: Draft a phased hiring plan (0 → 1 → 2 part-time employees) and run numbers with Evan this weekend.

artifacts.content:
Women in Food Biz – panel prep call

Description:
Prep call with organizers for the “Women in Food Biz: Scaling Without Burning Out” panel at the downtown library.

Talking points they requested:
- Short intro (2–3 sentences) on who I am and my role at Prairie Harvest Granola Co.
- How I moved from hobby baking to running a small business.
- Juggling parenting, markets, and mental health.

Draft role description to test on the call:
“I’m the **Owner & Operations/Production Manager** at Prairie Harvest Granola Co. I oversee everything from sourcing local oats and honey to scheduling bakes, packing orders, and handling wholesale accounts.”

Reminders:
- Mention that many of my early corporate clients were small business owners and HR managers I met through the Old Strathcona Business Association and my anxiety support group at the U of A Hospital outpatient program. That includes Melissa, the HR coordinator who ordered 200 branded snack packs for her engineering firm’s mental health awareness week after I opened up about struggling to keep the business going while dealing with panic attacks.
- Clarify that I’m still very hands‑on in production, even though I’m exploring hiring help for packaging and markets.
- Ask if they want a more formal title like “Owner & Operations Manager” on the event poster, or if “Owner & Head Granola Maker” is okay.

To‑do before call:
- Email them a 50‑word bio.  
- Send logo and 1–2 product photos.

metadata.title: Panel prep: confirm how to describe my role

artifacts.content:
From: Danielle Carter <danielle@prairieharvestgranola.ca>
To: Mark Liu <mark@rivercityaccounting.ca>
Date: 2026-01-12 09:18
Subject: Clarifying employee numbers for T4s & payroll setup

Hi Mark,

Thanks again for squeezing me in last week. I just want to make sure I’m filling out your onboarding form correctly for Prairie Harvest Granola Co.

Right now I don’t have any **full-time** staff in the traditional sense – it’s basically me plus a patchwork of help. I’ve got two regular part-time helpers for markets and packaging (both under 20 hours/week), and I sometimes bring in an extra casual person if there’s a big wholesale order. None of them are anywhere close to 30–40 hours, and I’m not offering benefits yet.

On your form it asks for “number of full-time employees” and “number of part-time employees.” Am I right that for full-time I should put **0** and then list **3** under part-time/casual? I just don’t want CRA to think I’m misclassifying anyone.

Also, one of the part-timers occasionally invoices me as a contractor for social media content instead of hourly shifts. Does that change which box I put her in for your payroll records, or do you just need her as a separate contractor line item?

Sorry for all the questions – this is my first year not just winging it with a spreadsheet, and I’m trying hard not to repeat the surprise tax bill situation from 2024.

Danielle
Owner, Prairie Harvest Granola Co.
Unit 302, 10745 83 Ave NW, Edmonton, AB

Date: 2026-02-03 14:42
Subject: Re: Production capacity & staffing details

Hi Alison,

I really appreciate you sending over the vendor questionnaire. I’ve filled out most of it, but I wanted to clarify the staffing question before I send it back.

At the moment, Prairie Harvest doesn’t have any employees who meet your definition of “full-time (35+ hours/week).” I’m the owner-operator, and I pay myself out of the business, but I don’t count that as a separate full-time employee line on forms like this.

On the production side, I have **two part-time packaging assistants** who rotate shifts in the shared kitchen, plus a **seasonal weekend helper** I bring on for the busier farmers’ market months. Between the three of them, I can comfortably double my usual output for limited periods, but the official full-time headcount for the business is still **zero**.

If you need a more formal breakdown, I can estimate average weekly hours per role. I’m also in talks with someone about a possible part-time position that could grow into full-time if the trial in your four stores goes well, but that won’t be in place before May.

Let me know if you’d like me to attach the draft production schedule I’ve sketched out for the Maple Pecan Prairie Blend and Honey Berry Sunrise launch.

Best,
Danielle
Prairie Harvest Granola Co.

Title: Meeting with BDC advisor – staffing & growth plan
Date: 2026-03-08 10:00–11:00
Location: BDC Edmonton Business Centre, 124 St
Attendees: Danielle Carter; Priya Singh (BDC Advisor)

Agenda:

1. Overview of Prairie Harvest Granola Co.
   - Current structure: sole proprietor
   - No full-time employees listed on payroll
   - Mix of casual and part-time help for markets and packaging

2. Staffing questions
   - Whether to formalize existing helpers as part-time employees vs. contractors
   - Pros and cons of hiring **first full-time staff member** vs. staying with **0 full-time employees** for another year
   - Impact of different scenarios on cash flow and ability to meet new wholesale demand

3. Financials & risk
   - Review of 2025 revenue and expenses
   - Discussion of emergency fund after 2025 car repair
   - How many part-time hours the business can sustain without moving to a full-time role

4. Action items
   - Priya to send sample staffing plan for micro food producers with 0–3 part-time employees
   - Danielle to model scenarios comparing: (a) keeping 0 full-time staff and adding one more part-timer; (b) converting one part-timer into 30-hour/week role; (c) delaying any full-time hire until after Capital Region Grocers’ trial period.

Notes:

Danielle wants to maintain small-batch feel and avoid locking into a full-time salary before revenue from new accounts is stable. Also worried about burnout if she doesn’t get some help soon.

metadata.title: Meeting with BDC advisor – staffing & growth plan

artifacts.content:
From: Evan Carter <evan.carter@gmail.com>
To: Danielle Carter <danielle@prairieharvestgranola.ca>
Date: 2026-01-28 21:07
Subject: Re: That hiring question from your insurance guy

Hey Dani,

Just circling back to what you asked over dinner about the insurance form.

If I remember right, the broker’s questionnaire had that section where you tick a box for how many full-time employees the business has (0, 1–5, 6–10, etc.). Since you don’t have anyone on staff doing 35–40 hours a week besides yourself, I’m pretty sure you’re supposed to check the **“0 full-time employees”** option.

You’ve got the Saturday market helper and the uni student who comes in to label bags, but both of them are very clearly part-time. Plus, your mom only helps out when she’s up from Red Deer, and you don’t pay her beyond gas money and feeding her granola.

I’d be careful not to overstate it just because you feel like the business is “real” now. From an insurance and liability standpoint, it’s better to be accurate about the fact that you’re still a one-woman show supported by a couple of part-timers, not a company with a full-time staff count.

If you want, I can scan the form and highlight the section I’m talking about. But I’d definitely avoid checking anything that implies even **one** official full-time employee when that’s not how you’re set up.

Love,
Evan

metadata.subject: Re: That hiring question from your insurance guy

Title: Call with Alberta Women Entrepreneurs – HR basics
Date: 2026-02-17 13:30–14:15
Location: Phone – Danielle’s cell
Attendees: Danielle Carter; AWE Advisor (TBD)

Description/Notes:

Purpose of the call is to walk through HR basics for very small businesses.

Key points Danielle wants to cover:

- How to report staffing levels when the business has **no full-time employees** on payroll.
- Whether the owner counts as staff on forms that ask for “number of full-time employees,” or if she should continue listing it as **0** until she formally hires someone.
- Best practices for onboarding 2–3 part-time helpers for packaging and farmers’ markets (contracts, payroll vs. contractor status, WCB, etc.).
- At what point (in terms of hours or revenue) it makes sense to consider creating a full-time position instead of juggling multiple casual roles.
- Any funding or grant programs that require a minimum of 2+ full-time staff, and whether she should wait before applying.

Background for advisor:

Prairie Harvest Granola Co. is a home-based and shared-kitchen micro food business. Danielle is cautious about taking on the responsibility of a full-time salary while the grocery trial is still in its early stages. She’s still recovering from the financial hit of a big car repair last year and wants to avoid overextending.

artifacts.content:
From: Danielle Carter <danielle@prairieharvestgranola.ca>
To: Sarah Ng <sarah.ng@capitalwellness.ca>
Date: 2026-02-09 09:18
Subject: Re: Vendor details for Capital Wellness Fair

Hi Sarah,

Thanks for sending over the vendor information form. I started filling it out this morning between oven timers and preschool drop-off.

On the question about staffing/headcount: Prairie Harvest Granola is still very lean. I don’t have any full-time staff on payroll yet – it’s basically me plus a rotating cast of helpers. My cousin Jess comes in on an as-needed basis around big market weekends to do packaging and labeling, but she’s strictly casual and logs hours only when we have a crunch. Evan helps with deliveries during school breaks, and my mom occasionally steps in when I’m slammed, but none of them are formal employees.

For the event itself, I’m planning to bring one part-time helper to manage samples and Square payments so I can talk to people about ingredients and allergy protocols. I also get steady repeat orders from parents in the peanut-free allergy support group I joined at the Stollery Children’s Hospital in April 2024 after Liam had a scary reaction to a store-bought granola bar at Garneau School; one of the moms, Jenna, posted in our private Facebook group that my School-Safe Crunch was the only granola she trusted, and since then about a dozen families from that group have become regular customers. Because of that, I’m expecting a lot of detailed ingredient questions at the fair.

Let me know if, for your records, you want me to round my staffing up to “2 part-time” or list it differently.

Best,
Danielle
Owner, Prairie Harvest Granola Co.

metadata.subject: Re: Vendor details for Capital Wellness Fair

artifacts.content:
From: Mark Delaney <mark.delaney@absmallbusiness.ca>
To: Danielle Carter <danielle@prairieharvestgranola.ca>
Date: 2026-01-18 14:42
Subject: Follow-up: Hiring & HR basics for microbusinesses

Hi Danielle,

Great chatting with you at the Alberta Small Business Growth Lab session last week. You’d asked whether Prairie Harvest Granola Co. was “big enough” to worry about formal HR policies.

Based on what you described – seasonal market help, your cousin doing a few shifts here and there, and your mom occasionally helping with the kids so you can bake – you’re still in the pre-employee phase in our books. Contractors, family volunteers, and occasional paid helpers don’t count as full-time employees for most of the government forms we discussed. When a business reports its number of full-time employees, they’re generally talking about people on payroll who work 30+ hours every week on an ongoing basis.

You mentioned you might bring on two part-time packaging assistants this spring if the specialty grocer trial goes well. If that happens, your “full-time employee” count will still be recorded as zero, even though your total headcount will effectively be three people working various hours.

Once you have at least three full-time staff, different rules kick in around written policies, vacation tracking, etc. You’re not there yet, so don’t feel pressured to over-formalize things.

If you’d like, I can send you a simple spreadsheet template for tracking hours for casual and part-time workers so you’re ready when you eventually convert someone to a full-time role.

Best regards,
Mark Delaney
Advisor, AB Small Business Growth Lab

metadata.subject: Follow-up: Hiring & HR basics for microbusinesses

Title: Strategy Call – Scaling Production & First Employee
Date: 2026-03-07 10:00–11:00
Location: Zoom (link in invite)
Attendees: Danielle Carter; Rachel Kim (Small Batch Business Coaching)

Agenda:

1. Quick recap (10 min)
   - Where Prairie Harvest Granola is at now: solo owner-operator, no full-time employees, mix of casual help from family and friends.
   - Current production capacity using shared Ritchie kitchen twice a week.

2. Staffing scenarios (25 min)
   - Option A: Stay lean with 2–3 regular part-time packaging staff during peak months only.
   - Option B: Transition one reliable helper to a 0.6 FTE role while keeping others as on-call.
   - Clarify how to report “number of full-time employees” on grant and loan applications when you technically have zero but rely heavily on part-time labour.

3. Customer base & demand forecasting (15 min)
   - Discuss steady repeat orders from the peanut-free allergy support group Danielle joined at the Stollery Children’s Hospital after Liam’s reaction to a store-bought granola bar at Garneau School.
   - Plan for back-to-school surges when about a dozen families from that group buy 6–8 bags of School-Safe Crunch at a time.

4. Action items (10 min)
   - Draft simple role description for part-time packaging help.
   - Create 12-month hiring timeline tied to wholesale trial results.
   - Prepare numbers for “headcount” questions on upcoming microbusiness grant application (currently zero full-time staff).

artifacts.content:
From: Danielle Carter <danielle@prairieharvestgranola.ca>
To: info@cityofedmonton.ca
Date: 2025-11-03 16:27
Subject: Clarification on "number of full-time employees" for business license renewal

Hello,

I’m renewing the business license for Prairie Harvest Granola Co. and got stuck on the section asking for the number of full-time employees.

My situation is a bit unusual. I’m the owner and do most of the production myself, but I don’t consider myself a full-time employee in the traditional sense because I don’t pay myself a fixed salary yet – I just draw from profits when cash flow allows. On top of that, I have:

- One regular part-time helper (10–12 hours/week) who assists with packaging near market dates.
- Occasional paid help from a university student around Christmas.
- Family support that is unpaid (my husband doing deliveries on PD days, my mom babysitting during big bake weeks).

I’m not sure whether I should list my full-time employee count as “0” or “2” (counting myself and my part-time helper). I definitely don’t have multiple full-time staff on payroll the way a café or restaurant would. Revenue is still modest, and I’m just trying to stay compliant without overstating my size.

Could you please confirm how a microbusiness like mine should report this? I want to be accurate for city records and any future inspections.

Thank you,

Danielle Marie Carter
Owner, Prairie Harvest Granola Co.
Unit 302, 10745 83 Ave NW
Edmonton, AB T6E 2E5

metadata.subject: Clarification on "number of full-time employees" for business license renewal
